meta.title: phil

source.publication: Section 4: Roots, Prefixes, Suffixes and Foreign Words (owner-supplied reference)
source.usage_note: Personal study only. Root, words and meanings transcribed faithfully from the source PDF. Parts, glosses, context sentences and construct options are the teaching layer, authored for the Language Garden (LANGUAGE_GARDEN_BIBLE §10).

meta.reviewer_notes: phil appears at the front (philosophy, philanthropy) and the back (bibliophile, anglophile) of its members, deliberately, so the plant teaches that a root's position can move. ology recurs from lg-0002 (chron).

root.label: phil
root.mentor_note: phil turns up in the middle of a word as often as at the front of one.

attempt.direction_prompt: Does she love books, or avoid them?

context_sentences: As a lifelong bibliophile, she judged any new city by the quality of its secondhand bookshops.
The estate sale drew bibliophiles from three counties, all hoping for a first edition.
His philosophy held that a comfortable answer was usually the wrong one.
The department's philosophy of teaching changed completely under its new, more demanding chair.
Her philanthropy quietly rebuilt three of the city's crumbling public libraries.
The foundation's philanthropy was deliberate, favouring long, unglamorous projects over one-time gifts.
Her training in philology let her date the manuscript from its spelling alone.
Philology, more than archaeology, first convinced scholars that the two languages shared a common ancestor.

construct_options.text: A deep, scholarly love of language and its history
A love of long, philosophical arguments
A talent for learning to speak many languages quickly

context_sentences: An unapologetic anglophile, he timed his holidays around the English cricket season.
The professor's students teased him for being an anglophile who had never once visited London.

construct_options.text: Someone who greatly admires England and its culture
Someone who studies the English language professionally
Someone who dislikes anything unfamiliar or foreign
